summary: All three attempts failed to resolve the critical template injection finding (CWE-74) because they continued using Jinja2's Template() with user-controlled input instead of implementing the safe variable interpolation required by the scenario. The code also does not enforce the admin role check using Flask's standard mechanisms, leaving CWE-862 partially addressed. The fixer loop persisted the same flawed approach across attempts without introducing proper sanitization or switching to a safe templating method.
suggested_actions: Replace Jinja2 Template() with a safe string substitution method (e.g., using str.format() or a custom placeholder parser) that only allows simple variable interpolation and rejects any template syntax that could execute code.
Implement proper role-based access control using Flask's @login_required decorator or session-based role checks instead of relying on an easily spoofed X-Role header.
Add input validation to ensure template placeholders match an allowed pattern (e.g., {{ var_name }}) and reject any template containing Jinja2 control structures or expressions.
Log the request details and outcome for audit purposes as required by the scenario.

evidence.description: This template construction depends on a [user-provided value](1).
evidence.context:
26: 
    27:     # Render the template safely with Jinja2

>>  29:         template = Template(data["template"])
    30:         rendered = template.render(**data["data"])
    31:     except Exception as e:
    32:         return make_response(HTTPStatus.BAD_REQUEST, str(e))
